title: Roast pork loin with crackling
subtitle: Scored skin, overnight dry, hot oven start
excerpt: Pork loin roasted with its skin on, the rind scored and dried overnight so it blisters into crisp crackling. Served with apple sauce.
sourceNotes:
Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136. Roast pork entries and crackling method.

Eliza Acton, Modern Cookery for Private Families (1845), Project Gutenberg. Roast pork with crisp rind.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Score and salt the rind the night before, refrigerate uncovered. The dry surface is what makes the crackling.
recipe.temperatureNote: fan oven; reduce to 180 after the initial 30-minute blast

glossaryTerms.definition: Leaving cooked meat in a warm place before carving, allowing the muscle fibres to relax and the juices to redistribute so the meat stays moist when cut.
The crisp, bubbly layer formed when pork rind is scored, dried and roasted at high heat. The score marks allow the rind to expand and blister without splitting.

content.text: Pork loin with crackling delivers two textures in one joint: tender, juicy meat inside and a crisp, salty rind on top. The crackling is the prize. Getting it right requires scored rind, a completely dry surface, and a very hot oven blast at the start.
The overnight dry-salt stage is the most important step. Salt draws moisture from the rind; a dry rind crisps rather than steams.
What you need
A sharp knife or a craft knife makes the cleanest score lines in the rind. Aim for lines 1 cm apart, cutting through the rind but not deep into the fat.
The night before
Score the rind in parallel lines about 1 cm apart, cutting through the skin and just into the fat beneath.
Pat the rind completely dry. Rub the sea salt flakes firmly into the score marks. Leave uncovered in the fridge overnight.
Method
Prepare for the oven
Take the pork from the fridge an hour before cooking. Pat the rind dry again with kitchen paper if any moisture has beaded on the surface.
Rub the flesh side and edges with the fine salt, pepper, and fennel seeds if using. Heat the oven to 230 degrees C fan.
Scatter the onion and Bramley apple across the base of the roasting pan and set the rack on top. Place the pork rind-side up on the rack.
Roast
Roast at 230 degrees C for 30 minutes to blister the rind into
crackling
. Watch carefully; if it is catching and darkening too fast, reduce the heat slightly.
Reduce to 180 degrees C and continue roasting until the probe reads 68 degrees C at the thickest point of the meat, away from any bone. This takes about 60 to 70 more minutes for a 1.8 kg joint.
Rest and carve
Transfer to a warm board and
rest
for 20 minutes. Do not cover the rind with foil or it will soften.
Cut down through the crackling between the score lines. Carve the meat into slices and serve with a piece of crackling on each plate.

items.symptom: The crackling is soft rather than crisp
items.cause: The rind was not dry enough, or the oven was not hot enough at the start.
items.fix: Overnight drying is essential. If the crackling is still soft after 30 minutes at 230 degrees C, turn the grill on for 5 minutes, watching closely.

items.symptom: The crackling is hard and tough rather than crunchy
items.cause: The score lines were too shallow, or the heat was not high enough to make the rind blister.
items.fix: Score firmly through the rind and top layer of fat. The initial blast needs to be a full 230 degrees C.
Pull at 68 to 70 degrees C and rest well. Loin dries out quickly if overcooked.

items.symptom: Rind is uneven: some crisp, some soft
items.cause: The joint was not sitting level in the tin.
items.fix: If the joint is uneven, prop the lower end with a wedge of onion so the rind sits level across the whole surface.

content.text: Variations

Pork belly gives even more generous crackling and a richer result. Score and dry the same way, but roast for three hours at 160 degrees C after the initial blast rather than probing for a set internal temperature.
Where this dish lives

Crackling has been the mark of a well-roasted pig in British cooking since at least the eighteenth century. Charles Lamb, in his Dissertation on Roast Pig, describes the discovery of crackling with mock-heroic reverence. Modern butchers score it as a matter of course; the cook just has to dry it properly.
